label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so the user is asking about this ethical dilemma where you're at a restaurant with bad service but still need to leave a tip. Let me try to break this down. First, I need to understand the different sides here. On one hand, societal norms say you should tip, maybe even a standard 15-20%, regardless of the service. But if the service was really bad, is it fair to still tip that much? People might feel wronged, especially if they paid a lot for food and the service didn't match up. Then there's the perspective of the waiter. Tips are a big part of their income, right? In many places, minimum wage for servers is lower, so they rely on tips to make a living. So if you don't tip, they might suffer. That's a point to consider—maybe the service was bad because they're overworked or underpaid, not laziness. Ethical principles... Maybe deontological ethics, like following a duty or rule. If the rule is to always tip, then you should follow
